<p>When treating for the arthritis-dermatitis syndrome, the provider can switch to an oral agent guided by antimicrobial susceptibility testing 24&ndash;48 hours after substantial clinical improvement, for a total treatment course of <span style="background-color: #ffff00"><span class="sr-only">start highlight</span>&gt;7 days<span class="sr-only">end highlight</span></span>.</p>
<h5>Treatment of Gonococcal Meningitis and Endocarditis</h5>
<table border="1px" cellpadding="5px">
<caption class="">Recommended Regimen for Gonococcal Meningitis and Endocarditis</caption>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><b>Recommended Regimen for Gonococcal Meningitis and Endocarditis</b></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><b>Ceftriaxone</b> 1&ndash;2 g IV <span style="background-color: #ffff00"><span class="sr-only">start highlight</span>every 24 hours<span class="sr-only">end highlight</span></span></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>If chlamydial infection has not been excluded, providers should treat for chlamydia with doxycycline 100 mg orally 2 times/day for 7 days.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p>No recent studies have been published regarding treatment of DGI involving the CNS or cardiovascular system. The duration of treatment for DGI in these situations has not been systematically studied and should be determined in consultation with an infectious disease specialist. Treatment for DGI should be guided by the results of antimicrobial susceptibility testing. Length of treatment should be determined based on clinical presentation. Therapy for meningitis should be continued with recommended parenteral therapy for 10&ndash;14 days. Parenteral antimicrobial therapy for endocarditis should be administered for &gt;4 weeks. Treatment of gonococcal perihepatitis should be managed in accordance with the recommendations for PID in these guidelines.</p>
<h5>Management of Sex Partners</h5>
<p>Gonococcal infection frequently is asymptomatic among sex partners of persons who have DGI. Providers should instruct patients to refer partners with whom they have had sexual contact during the previous 60 days for evaluation, testing, and presumptive treatment (see Gonococcal Infections, Management of Sex Partners).</p>
<h3 class="h3">Gonococcal Infection Among Neonates</h3>
<p>Prenatal screening and treatment of pregnant women for gonorrhea is the best method for preventing <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> infection among neonates. Gonococcal infection among neonates results from perinatal exposure to the mother&rsquo;s infected cervix. It is usually an acute illness that manifests 2&ndash;5 days after birth. Prevalence of infection among neonates depends on the prevalence of infection among pregnant women and whether pregnant women are screened and treated for gonorrhea during pregnancy. The most severe manifestations of <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> infection among neonates are ophthalmia neonatorum and sepsis, which can include arthritis and meningitis. Less severe manifestations include rhinitis, vaginitis, urethritis, and scalp infection at sites of previous fetal monitoring.</p>
<h4>Preventing Ophthalmia Neonatorum Caused by <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i></h4>
<p>Ocular prophylaxis and preventive gonorrhea screening and treatment of infected pregnant women are especially important because ophthalmia neonatorum can result in perforation of the globe of the eye and blindness (<i>903</i>). Ocular prophylaxis for gonococcal ophthalmia neonatorum has a long history of preventing sight-threatening gonococcal ocular infections. Cases in the United States are uncommon, which is likely attributable to gonorrhea screening programs for women, including pregnant women, that have contributed substantially to reduction in ophthalmia neonatorum (<i>904</i>). Neonatal ocular prophylaxis with erythromycin, the only agent available in the United States, is required by law in most states and is recommended because of safety, low cost, and ease of administration. It can contribute to preventing gonococcal blindness because not all pregnant women are screened for gonorrhea. The USPSTF recommends ocular prophylaxis with erythromycin ointment for all newborns &lt;24 hours after birth (<i>903</i>). In addition to continuing routine ocular prophylaxis, prevention should focus on prenatal screening for <i>N. gonorrhoeae,</i> including</p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>screening pregnant women at risk (e.g., women aged &lt;25 years and those aged &ge;25 years who have a new sex partner, more than one sex partner, a sex partner with concurrent partners, a sex partner who has an STI, or live in a community with high rates of gonorrhea) for <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> infection at the first prenatal visit;</li>
<li>treating all pregnant women with <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> infection during pregnancy and retesting in 3 months, in the third trimester or at time of delivery (sex partners should be tested and treated);</li>
<li>retesting pregnant women in the third trimester who initially tested negative but remained at increased risk for acquiring infection (e.g., women aged &lt;25 years and those aged &ge;25 years who have a new sex partner, more than one sex partner, a sex partner with concurrent partners, a sex partner who has an STI, or live in a community with high rates of gonorrhea); and</li>
<li>screening for gonorrhea at delivery for women not tested during pregnancy and at risk for infection (e.g., women aged &lt;25 years and those aged &ge;25 years who have a new sex partner, more than one sex partner, a sex partner with concurrent partners, a sex partner who has an STI, or live in a community with high rates of gonorrhea) or received no prenatal care; providers caring for the mother and the newborn should communicate to ensure follow-up on the results of laboratory tests performed at delivery, and if positive, prompt appropriate treatment of the newborn and mother.</li>
</ul>
<p>Erythromycin is the only ophthalmic ointment recommended for use among neonates. Silver nitrate and tetracycline ophthalmic ointments are no longer manufactured in the United States, bacitracin is ineffective, and povidone iodine has not been studied adequately (<i>905</i>,<i>906</i>). Gentamicin ophthalmic ointment has been associated with severe ocular reactions (<i>907</i>,<i>908</i>). If erythromycin ointment is unavailable, infants at risk for exposure to <i>N. gonorrhoeae,</i> especially those born to a mother at risk for gonococcal infection or with no prenatal care, can be administered ceftriaxone 25&ndash;50 mg/kg body weight IV or IM, not to exceed 250 mg in a single dose.</p>
<table border="1px" cellpadding="5px">
<caption class="">Recommended Regimen to Prevent Ophthalmia Neonatorum Caused by <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i></caption>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><b>Recommended Regimen to Prevent Ophthalmia Neonatorum Caused by <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i></b></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><b>Erythromycin</b> <b>0.5% ophthalmic ointment</b> in each eye in a single application at birth</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p>Erythromycin ophthalmic ointment should be instilled into both eyes of neonates as soon as possible after delivery, regardless of whether they are delivered vaginally or by cesarean delivery. Ideally, ointment should be applied by using single-use tubes or ampules rather than multiple-use tubes. If prophylaxis is delayed (i.e., not administered in the delivery room), a monitoring system should be established to ensure that all newborns receive prophylaxis &lt;24 hours after delivery.</p>
<h5>Diagnostic Considerations</h5>
<p>Newborns at increased risk for gonococcal ophthalmia include those who did not receive ophthalmic prophylaxis and whose mothers had no prenatal care, have a history of STIs during pregnancy, or have a history of substance misuse. Gonococcal ophthalmia is strongly suspected when intracellular gram-negative diplococci are identified on Gram stain of conjunctival exudate, justifying presumptive treatment for gonorrhea after appropriate cultures and antimicrobial susceptibility testing for <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> are performed. Presumptive treatment for <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> might be indicated for newborns at increased risk for gonococcal ophthalmia who have increased WBCs (no GNID) in a Gram-stained smear of conjunctival exudate. Nongonococcal causes of neonatal ophthalmia include <i>Moraxella catarrhalis</i> and other <i>Neisseria</i> species, which are organisms that are indistinguishable from <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> on Gram-stained smear but can be differentiated in the microbiology laboratory.</p>
<h5>Treatment of Gonococcal Ophthalmia Neonatorum</h5>
<table border="1px" cellpadding="5px">
<caption class="">Recommended Regimen for Gonococcal Ophthalmia Neonatorum</caption>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><b>Recommended Regimen for Gonococcal Ophthalmia Neonatorum</b></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><b>Ceftriaxone</b> 25&ndash;50 mg/kg body weight IV or IM in a single dose, not to exceed 250 mg</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p>One dose of ceftriaxone is adequate therapy for gonococcal ophthalmia. Ceftriaxone should be administered cautiously to neonates with hyperbilirubinemia, especially those born prematurely. Cefotaxime 100 mg/kg body weight IV or IM as a single dose can be administered for those neonates unable to receive ceftriaxone because of simultaneous administration of IV calcium. Topical antibiotic therapy alone is inadequate and unnecessary if systemic treatment is administered.</p>
<h5>Other Management Considerations</h5>
<p>Chlamydial testing should be performed simultaneously from the inverted eyelid specimen (see Ophthalmia Neonatorum Caused by <i>C. trachomatis</i>). Newborns who have gonococcal ophthalmia should be evaluated for signs of disseminated infection (e.g., sepsis, arthritis, and meningitis). Newborns who have gonococcal ophthalmia should be managed in consultation with an infectious disease specialist.</p>
<h5>Management of Mothers and Their Sex Partners</h5>
<p>Mothers of newborns with ophthalmia neonatorum caused by <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> should be evaluated, tested, and presumptively treated for gonorrhea, along with their sex partners (see Gonococcal Infection Among Adolescents and Adults).</p>
<h4>Disseminated Gonococcal Infection and Gonococcal Scalp Abscesses Among Neonates</h4>
<p>DGI might present as sepsis, arthritis, or meningitis and is a rare complication of neonatal gonococcal infection. Localized gonococcal infection of the scalp can result from fetal monitoring through scalp electrodes. Detecting gonococcal infection among neonates who have sepsis, arthritis, meningitis, or scalp abscesses requires cultures of blood, CSF, or joint aspirate. Specimens obtained from the conjunctiva, vagina, oropharynx, and rectum are useful for identifying the primary site or sites of infection. Antimicrobial susceptibility testing of all isolates should be performed. Positive Gram-stained smears of abscess exudate, CSF, or joint aspirate provide a presumptive basis for initiating treatment for <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i>.</p>
<h5>Treatment</h5>
<table border="1px" cellpadding="5px">
<caption class="">Recommended Regimens for Disseminated Gonococcal Infection Among Neonates</caption>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><b>Recommended Regimens for Disseminated Gonococcal Infection Among Neonates</b></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><b>Ceftriaxone </b>25&ndash;50 mg/kg body weight/day IV or IM in a single daily dose for 7 days, with a duration of 10&ndash;14 days if meningitis is documented</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><i>or</i></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><b>Cefotaxime </b>25 mg/kg body weight/day IV or IM every 12 hours for 7 days, with a duration of 10&ndash;14 days if meningitis is documented</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p>Ceftriaxone should be administered cautiously to neonates with hyperbilirubinemia, especially those born prematurely. Cefotaxime 100 mg/kg body weight IV or IM as a single dose can be administered for those neonates unable to receive ceftriaxone because of simultaneous administration of IV calcium.</p>
<h5>Other Management Considerations</h5>
<p>Chlamydial testing should be performed simultaneously among neonates with gonococcal infection (see Chlamydial Infection Among Neonates). Neonates who have DGI should be managed in consultation with an infectious disease specialist.</p>
<h5>Management of Mothers and Their Sex Partners</h5>
<p>Mothers of newborns who have DGI or scalp abscesses caused by <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> should be evaluated, tested, and presumptively treated for gonorrhea, along with their sex partners (see Gonococcal Infection Among Adolescents and Adults).</p>
<h4>Neonates Born to Mothers Who Have Gonococcal Infection</h4>
<p>Neonates born to mothers who have untreated gonorrhea are at high risk for infection. Neonates should be tested for gonorrhea at exposed sites (e.g., conjunctiva, vagina, rectum, and oropharynx) and treated presumptively for gonorrhea.</p>
<h5>Treatment in the Absence of Signs of Gonococcal Infection</h5>
<table border="1px" cellpadding="5px">
<caption class="">Recommended Regimen for Neonates Without Signs of Gonococcal Infection</caption>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><b>Recommended Regimen for Neonates Without Signs of Gonococcal Infection</b></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><b>Ceftriaxone</b> <span style="background-color: #ffff00"><span class="sr-only">start highlight</span>20&ndash;50 mg/kg<span class="sr-only">end highlight</span></span> body weight IV or IM in a single dose, not to exceed 250 mg</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<h5>Other Management Considerations</h5>
<p>Ceftriaxone should be administered cautiously to neonates with hyperbilirubinemia, especially those born prematurely. Cefotaxime 100 mg/kg body weight IV or IM as a single dose can be administered for those neonates unable to receive ceftriaxone because of simultaneous administration of IV calcium. Age-appropriate chlamydial testing should be performed simultaneously among neonates with gonococcal infection (see Chlamydial Infection Among Neonates). Follow-up examination is not required.</p>
<h5>Management of Mothers and Their Sex Partners</h5>
<p>Mothers who have gonorrhea and their sex partners should be evaluated, tested, and presumptively treated for gonorrhea (see Gonococcal Infection Among Adolescents and Adults).</p>
<h3 class="h3">Gonococcal Infection Among Infants and Children</h3>
<p>Sexual abuse is the most frequent cause of gonococcal infection among infants and children (see Sexual Assault or Abuse of Children). For preadolescent girls, vaginitis is the most common manifestation of this infection; gonococcal-associated PID after vaginal infection can be less common among preadolescents than adults. Among sexually abused children, anorectal and pharyngeal infections with <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> are frequently asymptomatic.</p>
<h4>Diagnostic Considerations</h4>
<p>Culture can be used to test urogenital and extragenital sites for girls and boys. NAAT can be used to test for <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> from vaginal and urine specimens from girls and urine for boys (see Sexual Assault or Abuse of Children). Although data regarding NAAT from extragenital sites (rectum and pharynx) among children are more limited, and performance is test dependent, no evidence supports that performance of NAAT for detection of <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> among children differs from that among adults (<i>553</i>). Because of the implications of a <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> diagnosis in a child, only validated FDA-cleared NAAT assays should be used with extragenital specimens. Consultation with an expert is necessary before using NAAT to minimize the possibility of cross-reaction with nongonococcal <i>Neisseria</i> species and other commensals (e.g., <i>N. meningitidis</i>, <i>Neisseria sicca</i>, <i>Neisseria lactamica</i>, <i>Neisseria cinerea</i>, or <i>M. catarrhalis</i>) and to ensure correct interpretation of results.</p>
<p>Gram stains are inadequate for evaluating prepubertal children for gonorrhea and should not be used to diagnose or exclude gonorrhea. If evidence of DGI exists, gonorrhea culture and antimicrobial susceptibility testing should be obtained from relevant clinical sites (see Disseminated Gonococcal Infection).</p>
<table border="1px" cellpadding="5px">

</tr>
<tr>
<td><b>Ceftriaxone</b> 25&ndash;50 mg/kg body weight IV or IM in a single dose, not to exceed 250 mg IM</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

<h4>Other Management Considerations</h4>
<p>Follow-up cultures are unnecessary. Only parenteral cephalosporins (i.e., ceftriaxone) are recommended for use among children. All children identified as having gonococcal infections should be tested for <i>C. trachomatis</i>, syphilis, and HIV (see Sexual Assault or Abuse of Children).</p>
</div>
</div><div class="order-4"><p class="text-right"><a href="#">Top</a></p></div><div class="order-4 w-100"><div>
<h2><a id="mycoplasmagenitalium" class="onThisPageAnchor" title="Mycoplasma genitalium"></a><i>Mycoplasma genitalium</i></h2>
<p><i>M. genitalium</i> causes symptomatic and asymptomatic urethritis among men and is the etiology of approximately 15%&ndash;20% of NGU, 20%&ndash;25% of nonchlamydial NGU, and 40% of persistent or recurrent urethritis (<i>697</i>,<i>909</i>,<i>910</i>). Infection with <i>C. trachomatis</i> is common in selected geographic areas (<i>911</i>&ndash;<i>913</i>), although <i>M. genitalium</i> is often the sole pathogen. Data are insufficient to implicate <i>M. genitalium</i> infection with chronic complications among men (e.g., epididymitis, prostatitis, or infertility). The consequences of asymptomatic infection with <i>M. genitalium</i> among men are unknown.</p>
<p>Among women, <i>M. genitalium</i> has been associated with cervicitis, PID, preterm delivery, spontaneous abortion, and infertility, with an approximately twofold increase in the risk for these outcomes among women infected with <i>M. genitalium</i> (<i>766</i>)<i>. M. genitalium</i> infections among women are also frequently asymptomatic, and the consequences associated with asymptomatic <i>M. genitalium</i> infection are unknown.</p>
<p><i>M. genitalium</i> can be detected among 10%&ndash;30% of women with clinical cervicitis (<i>767</i>,<i>770</i>,<i>772</i>,<i>914</i>&ndash;<i>916</i>). The existing evidence between <i>M. genitalium</i> and cervicitis is mostly supportive of a causal association. Elevated proinflammatory cytokines have been demonstrated among women with <i>M. genitalium</i>, with return to baseline levels after clearance of the pathogen (<i>917</i>)<i>.</i></p>
<p><i>M. genitalium</i> is identified in the cervix or endometrium of women with PID more often than in women without PID (<i>918</i>&ndash;<i>924</i>). Prevalence of <i>M. genitalium</i> among women with PID ranges from 4% to 22% (<i>925</i>,<i>926</i>) and was reported as 60% in one study of women with postabortal PID (<i>918</i>). The association with PID is supported by early studies among nonhuman primates that determined that endosalpingitis develops after inoculation with <i>M. genitalium</i> (<i>927</i>). Recent studies evaluating the lower and upper genital tract using highly sensitive <i>M. genitalium</i> NAAT assays or the role of <i>M. genitalium</i> in histologically defined endometritis have reported significantly elevated risk for PID (<i>928</i>). However, most studies of <i>M. genitalium</i> and PID, even those that controlled extensively for other infections and behavioral and biologic risk, are cross-sectional. The few prospective studies that have evaluated the role of <i>M. genitalium</i> in establishing subsequent PID demonstrated increased PID risk; however, these were not statistically significant associations, often because of a lack of statistical power. No clinical trial data are available that demonstrate that treating <i>M. genitalium</i> cervical infection prevents development of PID or endometritis. Although data regarding the benefits of testing women with PID for <i>M. genitalium</i> and the importance of directing treatment against this organism are limited, the associations of <i>M. genitalium</i> with cervicitis and PID in cross-sectional studies using NAAT testing are consistent (<i>928</i>).</p>
<p>Data from case-control serologic studies (<i>929</i>&ndash;<i>931</i>) and a meta-analysis of clinical studies (<i>766</i>) indicate a potential role in causing infertility. However, seroassays are suboptimal and inconclusive. Similarly, evidence for a role for <i>M. genitalium</i> infection during pregnancy as a cause of perinatal complications, including preterm delivery, spontaneous abortion, or low birthweight, are conflicting because evidence is insufficient to attribute cause (<i>766</i>,<i>932</i>&ndash;<i>934</i>). Data are limited regarding ectopic pregnancy and neonatal <i>M. genitalium</i> infection (<i>935</i>,<i>936</i>).</p>
<p>Rectal infection with <i>M. genitalium</i> has been reported among 1%&ndash;26% of MSM (<i>937</i>&ndash;<i>940</i>) and among 3% of women (<i>941</i>). Rectal infections often are asymptomatic, although higher prevalence of <i>M. genitalium</i> has been reported among men with rectal symptoms. Similarly, although asymptomatic <i>M. genitalium</i> has been detected in the pharynx, no evidence exists of it causing oropharyngeal symptoms or systemic disease.</p>
<p>Urogenital <i>M. genitalium</i> infection is associated with HIV among both men and women (<i>942</i>&ndash;<i>944</i>); however, the data are from case-control and cross-sectional studies. Risk for HIV infection is increased among women with <i>M. genitalium</i>, and evidence indicates that HIV shedding occurs more often among persons with <i>M. genitalium</i> and HIV infection who are not taking ART than among persons without <i>M. genitalium</i> (<i>942</i>,<i>944</i>).</p>
<h3 class="h3">Antimicrobial Resistance</h3>
<p>Resistance to azithromycin has been rapidly increasing and has been confirmed in multiple studies. Prevalence of molecular markers for macrolide resistance, which highly correlates with treatment failure, ranges from 44% to 90% in the United States, Canada, Western Europe, and Australia (<i>697</i>,<i>702</i>,<i>945</i>&ndash;<i>953</i>). Treatment with azithromycin alone has been reported to select for resistance (<i>705</i>,<i>954</i>,<i>955</i>), with treatment of macrolide-susceptible infections with a 1-g dose of azithromycin resulting in selection of resistant-strain populations in 10%&ndash;12% of cases. The prevalence of quinolone resistance markers is much lower (<i>697</i>,<i>956</i>&ndash;<i>959</i>). The first clinical treatment failures after moxifloxacin were associated specifically with the S83I mutation in the <i>parC</i> gene (<i>954</i>,<i>960</i>). Prevalence of the S83I mutation in the United States ranges from 0% to 15% (<i>947</i>); however, correlation with fluoroquinolone treatment failure is less consistent than that with mutations associated with macrolide resistance (<i>953</i>,<i>961</i>,<i>962</i>). Clinically relevant quinolone resistance often is associated with coexistent macrolide resistance (<i>954</i>).</p>
<h3 class="h3">Diagnostic Considerations</h3>
<p><i>M. genitalium</i> is an extremely slow-growing organism. Culture can take up to 6 months, and technical laboratory capacity is limited to research settings. NAAT for <i>M. genitalium</i> is FDA cleared for use with urine and urethral, penile meatal, endocervical, and vaginal swab samples (<a href="https://www.hologic.com/package-inserts/diagnostic-products/aptima-mycoplasma-genitalium-assay" class="tp-link-policy" rel="noreferrer" data-domain-ext="com">https://www.hologic.com/package-inserts/diagnostic-products/aptima-mycoplasma-genitalium-assay<span aria-label="external icon" class="sr-only" role="img"></span><span class="fi cdc-icon-external x16 fill-external" alt="" aria-hidden="true"></span></a>). Molecular tests for macrolide (i.e., azithromycin) or quinolone (i.e., moxifloxacin) resistance markers are not commercially available in the United States. However, molecular assays that incorporate detection of mutations associated with macrolide resistance are under evaluation.</p>
<p>Men with recurrent NGU should be tested for <i>M. genitalium</i> using an FDA-cleared NAAT. If resistance testing is available, it should be performed and the results used to guide therapy. Women with recurrent cervicitis should be tested for <i>M. genitalium</i>, and testing should be considered among women with PID. Testing should be accompanied with resistance testing, if available. Screening of asymptomatic <i>M. genitalium</i> infection among women and men or extragenital testing for <i>M. genitalium</i> is not recommended. In clinical practice, if testing is unavailable, <i>M. genitalium</i> should be suspected in cases of persistent or recurrent urethritis or cervicitis and considered for PID.</p>
<h3 class="h3">Treatment</h3>
<p><i>M. genitalium</i> lacks a cell wall, and thus antibiotics targeting cell-wall biosynthesis (e.g., &szlig;-lactams including penicillins and cephalosporins) are ineffective against this organism. Because of the high rates of macrolide resistance with treatment failures (<i>707</i>) and efficient selection of additional resistance, a 1-g dose of azithromycin should not be used.</p>
<p>Two-stage therapy approaches, ideally using resistance-guided therapy, are recommended for treatment. Resistance-guided therapy has demonstrated cure rates of &gt;90% and should be used whenever possible (<i>759</i>,<i>963</i>); however, it requires access to macrolide-resistance testing. As part of this approach, doxycycline is provided as initial empiric therapy, which reduces the organism load and facilitates organism clearance, followed by macrolide-sensitive <i>M.</i> <i>genitalium</i> infections treated with high-dose azithromycin; macrolide-resistant infections are treated with moxifloxacin (<i>964</i>,<i>965</i>).</p>
<table border="1px" cellpadding="5px">
<caption class="">Recommended Regimens if <i>M. genitalium</i> Resistance Testing Is Available</caption>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><b>Recommended Regimens if <i>M. genitalium</i> Resistance Testing Is Available</b></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><b>If macrolide sensitive: Doxycycline </b>100 mg orally 2 times/day for 7 days, followed by <b>azithromycin</b> 1 g orally initial dose, followed by 500 mg orally once daily for 3 additional days (2.5 g total)</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><b>If macrolide resistant: Doxycycline </b>100 mg orally 2 times/day for 7 days followed by <b>moxifloxacin</b> 400 mg orally once daily for 7 days</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<table border="1px" cellpadding="5px">
<caption class="">Recommended Regimen if <i>M. genitalium</i> Resistance Testing Is Not Available</caption>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><b>Recommended Regimen if <i>M. genitalium</i> Resistance Testing Is Not Available</b></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><b>If <i>M. genitalium </i>is detected by an FDA-cleared NAAT: Doxycycline</b> 100 mg orally 2 times/day for 7 days, followed by <b>moxifloxacin</b> 400 mg orally once daily for 7 days</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p>Although the majority of <i>M. genitalium</i> strains are sensitive to moxifloxacin, resistance has been reported, and adverse side effects and cost should be considered with this regimen. In settings without access to resistance testing and when moxifloxacin cannot be used, an alternative regimen can be considered, based on limited data: doxycycline 100 mg orally 2 times/day for 7 days, followed by azithromycin (1 g orally on day 1 followed by 500 mg once daily for 3 days) and a test of cure 21 days after completion of therapy (<i>963</i>). Because of the high prevalence of macrolide resistance and high likelihood of treatment failure, this regimen should be used only when a test of cure is possible, and no other alternatives exist. If symptomatic treatment failure or a positive test of cure occurs after this regimen, expert consultation is recommended. Data are limited regarding use of minocycline in instances of treatment failure (<i>966</i>).</p>
<p>Recommended PID treatment regimens are not effective against <i>M. genitalium</i>. Initial empiric therapy for PID, which includes doxycycline 100 mg orally 2 times/day for 14 days, should be provided at the time of presentation for care. If <i>M. genitalium</i> is detected, a regimen of moxifloxacin 400 mg orally once daily for 14 days has been effective in eradicating the organism. Nevertheless, no data have been published that assess the benefits of testing women with PID for <i>M. genitalium,</i> and the importance of directing treatment against this organism is unknown.</p>
<h3 class="h3">Follow-Up</h3>
<p>Test of cure is not recommended for asymptomatic persons who received treatment with a recommended regimen. In settings in which <i>M. genitalium</i> testing is available, persons with persistent urethritis, cervicitis, or PID accompanied by detection of <i>M. genitalium</i> should be treated with moxifloxacin.</p>
<h3 class="h3">Management of Sex Partners</h3>
<p>Recent studies report a high concordance of <i>M. genitalium</i> among partners of males, females, and MSM; however, no studies have determined whether reinfection is reduced with partner treatment (<i>940</i>,<i>967</i>,<i>968</i>). Sex partners of patients with symptomatic <i>M. genitalium</i> infection can be tested, and those with a positive test can be treated to possibly reduce the risk for reinfection. If testing the partner is not possible, the antimicrobial regimen that was provided to the patient can be provided.</p>
<h3 class="h3">Special Considerations</h3>
<h4>HIV Infection</h4>
<p>Persons who have <i>M. genitalium</i> and HIV infection should receive the same treatment regimen as those persons without HIV.</p>
</div>
</div><div class="order-4"><p class="text-right"><a href="#">Top</a></p></div><div class="order-4 w-100"><div>
<h2><a id="diseasescharacterizedbyvulvovaginalitching,burning,irritation,odor,ordischarge" class="onThisPageAnchor" title="Diseases Characterized by Vulvovaginal Itching, Burning, Irritation, Odor, or Discharge"></a>Diseases Characterized by Vulvovaginal Itching, Burning, Irritation, Odor, or Discharge</h2>
<p>The majority of women will have a vaginal infection, characterized by discharge, itching, burning, or odor, during their lifetime. With the availability of complementary and alternative therapies and over-the-counter medications for candidiasis, symptomatic women often seek these products before or in addition to an evaluation by a medical provider.</p>
<p>Obtaining a medical history alone has been reported to be insufficient for accurate diagnosis of vaginitis and can lead to inappropriate administration of medication (<i>969</i>). Therefore, a careful history, examination, and laboratory testing to determine the etiology of any vaginal symptoms are warranted. Information regarding sexual behaviors and practices, sex of sex partners, menses, vaginal hygiene practices (e.g., douching), and self-treatment with oral and intravaginal medications or other products should be elicited. The infections most frequently associated with vaginal symptoms are BV (i.e., replacement of the vaginal flora by an overgrowth of anaerobic bacteria including <i>G. vaginalis</i>, <i>Prevotella</i> <i>bivia</i>, <i>A. vaginae</i>, <i>Megasphaera</i> type 1, and numerous other fastidious or uncultivated anaerobes), trichomoniasis, and vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC). Cervicitis can also cause an abnormal vaginal discharge. Although VVC is usually not sexually transmitted, it is included in this section because it is frequently diagnosed among women who have vaginal symptoms or are being evaluated for an STI.</p>
<p>Multiple diagnostic methods are available for identifying the etiology of vaginal symptoms. Clinical laboratory testing can identify the vaginitis cause in the majority of women and is discussed in detail in the sections of this report dedicated to each condition. In the clinician&rsquo;s office, the cause of vaginal symptoms can often be determined by pH, a potassium hydroxide (KOH) test, and microscopic examination of a wet mount of fresh samples of vaginal discharge. The pH of the vaginal secretions can be measured by pH paper; an elevated pH (i.e., &gt;4.5) is common with BV or trichomoniasis (although trichomoniasis can also be present with a normal vaginal pH). Because pH testing is not highly specific, vaginal discharge should be further examined microscopically by first diluting one sample in 1 or 2 drops of 0.9% normal saline solution on one slide and a second sample in 10% KOH solution (samples that emit an amine odor immediately upon application of KOH suggest BV or trichomoniasis). Coverslips are then placed on the slides, and they are examined under a microscope at low and high power. The saline-solution specimen might display motile trichomonads or clue cells (i.e., epithelial cells with borders obscured by small anaerobic bacteria), which are characteristic of BV. The KOH specimen typically is used to identify hyphae or blastospores observed with candidiasis. However, absence of trichomonads in saline or fungal elements in KOH samples does not rule out these infections because the sensitivity of microscopy is approximately 50% compared with NAAT (trichomoniasis) or culture (yeast) (<i>670</i>). Presence of WBCs without evidence of trichomonads or yeast might also indicate cervicitis (see Cervicitis).</p>
<p>In settings where pH paper, KOH, and microscopy are unavailable, a broad range of clinical laboratory tests, described in the diagnosis section for each disease, can be used. Presence of objective signs of vulvovaginal inflammation in the absence of vaginal pathogens after laboratory testing indicates the possibility of mechanical, chemical, allergic, or other noninfectious causes of vulvovaginal signs or symptoms. For women with persistent symptoms and no clear etiology, referral to a specialist should be considered.</p>
<h3 class="h3">Bacterial Vaginosis</h3>
<p>BV is a vaginal dysbiosis resulting from replacement of normal hydrogen peroxide and lactic-acid&ndash;producing <i>Lactobacillus</i> species in the vagina with high concentrations of anaerobic bacteria, including <i>G. vaginalis</i>, <i>Prevotella</i> species, <i>Mobiluncus</i> species, <i>A. vaginae</i>, and other BV-associated bacteria. A notable feature is the appearance of a polymicrobial biofilm on vaginal epithelial cells (<i>970</i>). Certain women experience transient vaginal microbial changes, whereas others experience them for longer intervals (<i>971</i>). BV is a highly prevalent condition and the most common cause of vaginal discharge worldwide (<i>972</i>). However, in a nationally representative survey, the majority of women with BV were asymptomatic (<i>310</i>).</p>
<p>BV is associated with having multiple male sex partners, female partners, sexual relationships with more than one person (<i>973</i>), a new sex partner, lack of condom use (<i>974</i>), douching (<i>975</i>,<i>976</i>), and HSV-2 seropositivity (<i>977</i>). Male circumcision reduces the risk for BV among women (<i>978</i>). In addition, BV prevalence increases during menses (<i>979</i>,<i>980</i>). Women who have never been sexually active are rarely affected (<i>981</i>). The cause of the microbial alteration that precipitates BV is not fully understood, and whether BV results from acquisition of a single sexually transmitted pathogen is unknown. BV prevalence has been reported to increase among women with copper-containing IUDs (<i>972</i>,<i>982</i>). Hormonal contraception does not increase risk for BV (<i>983</i>) and might protect against BV development (<i>983</i>,<i>984</i>). Vitamin D deficiency has not been reported to be a risk factor for BV (<i>985</i>).</p>
<p>Women with BV are at increased risk for STI acquisition, such as HIV, <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i>, <i>C. trachomatis</i>, <i>T. vaginalis</i> (<i>977</i>), <i>M. genitalium</i> (<i>986</i>), HPV (<i>987</i>), and HSV-2 (<i>988</i>); complications after gynecologic surgery; complications of pregnancy; and recurrence of BV (<i>971</i>,<i>989</i>&ndash;<i>991</i>). BV also increases HIV infection acquisition (<i>992</i>) because specific BV-associated bacteria can increase susceptibility to HIV (<i>993</i>,<i>994</i>) and the risk for HIV transmission to male sex partners (<i>187</i>). Evaluation of short-term valacyclovir suppression among women with HSV-2 did not decrease the risk for BV, despite effective suppression of HSV-2 (<i>995</i>).</p>
<p>Although BV-associated bacteria can be identified on male genitalia (<i>996</i>,<i>997</i>), treatment of male sex partners has not been beneficial in preventing the recurrence of BV (<i>998</i>). Among WSW, a high level of BV concordance occurs between sex partners (<i>292</i>); however, no studies have evaluated treatment of female sex partners of WSW to prevent BV recurrence.</p>
<h4>Diagnostic Considerations</h4>
<p>BV can be diagnosed by using clinical criteria (i.e., Amsel&rsquo;s diagnostic criteria) (<i>999</i>) or by determining the Nugent score from a vaginal Gram stain (<i>1000</i>). Vaginal Gram stain, considered the reference standard laboratory method for diagnosing BV, is used to determine the relative concentration of lactobacilli (i.e., long gram-positive rods), small gram-negative and gram-variable rods (i.e., <i>G. vaginalis</i> or <i>Bacteroides</i>), and curved gram-negative rods (i.e., <i>Mobiluncus</i>) characteristic of BV. A Nugent score of 0&ndash;3 is consistent with a <i>Lactobacillus</i>-predominant vaginal microbiota, 4&ndash;6 with intermediate microbiota (emergence of <i>G. vaginalis</i>), and 7&ndash;10 with BV. Clinical diagnosis of BV by Amsel criteria requires at least three of the following four symptoms or signs:</p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>Homogeneous, thin discharge (milklike consistency) that smoothly coats the vaginal walls</li>
<li>Clue cells (e.g., vaginal epithelial cells studded with adherent bacteria) on microscopic examination</li>
<li>pH of vaginal fluid &gt;4.5</li>
<li>A fishy odor of vaginal discharge before or after addition of 10% KOH (i.e., the whiff test)</li>
</ul>
<p>Detection of at least three Amsel criteria has been correlated with results by Gram stain (<i>1001</i>). The sensitivity and specificity of the Amsel criteria are 37%&ndash;70% and 94%&ndash;99%, respectively, compared with the Nugent score (<i>1002</i>).</p>
<p>In addition to the Amsel criteria, multiple POC tests are available for BV diagnosis. The Osom BV Blue test (Sekisui Diagnostics) detects vaginal sialidase activity (<i>1003</i>,<i>1004</i>). The Affirm VP III (Becton Dickinson) is an oligonucleotide probe test that detects high concentrations of <i>G. vaginalis</i> nucleic acids (&gt;5 x 10<sup>5</sup> CFU of <i>G. vaginalis/</i>mL of vaginal fluid) for diagnosing BV, <i>Candida</i> species, and <i>T. vaginalis.</i> This test has been reported to be most useful for symptomatic women in conjunction with vaginal pH measurement and presence of amine odor (sensitivity of 97%); specificity is 81% compared with Nugent. Finally, the FemExam Test Card (Cooper Surgical) measures vaginal pH, presence of trimethylamine (a metabolic by-product of <i>G. vaginalis</i>), and proline aminopeptidase (<i>1005</i>). Sensitivity is 91% and specificity is 61%, compared with Nugent. This test has primarily been studied in resource-poor settings (<i>1005</i>), and although it has been reported to be beneficial compared with syndromic management, it is not a preferred diagnostic method for BV diagnosis.</p>
<p>Multiple BV NAATs are available for BV diagnosis among symptomatic women (<i>1002</i>). These tests are based on detection of specific bacterial nucleic acids and have high sensitivity and specificity for BV (i.e., <i>G. vaginalis</i>, <i>A. vaginae</i>, BVAB2, or <i>Megasphaera</i> type 1) (<i>1006</i>) and certain lactobacilli (i.e., <i>Lactobacillus crispatus</i>, <i>Lactobacillus jensenii</i>, and <i>Lactobacillus gasseri</i>). They can be performed on either clinician- or self-collected vaginal specimens with results available in &lt;24 hours, depending on the availability of the molecular diagnostic platform (<i>1002</i>). Five quantitative multiplex PCR assays are available: Max Vaginal Panel (Becton Dickinson) (<i>1007</i>), Aptima BV (Hologic), NuSwab VG (LabCorp) (<i>1008</i>), OneSwab BV Panel PCR with Lactobacillus Profiling by qPCR (Medical Diagnostic Laboratories) (<i>1009</i>), and SureSwab BV (Quest Diagnostics). Two of these assays are FDA cleared (BD Max Vaginal Panel and Aptima BV), and the other three are laboratory-developed tests.</p>
<p>The Max Vaginal Panel provides results by an algorithmic analysis of molecular DNA detection of <i>Lactobacillus</i> species (<i>L. crispatus</i> and <i>L. jensenii</i>) in addition to <i>G. vaginalis</i>, <i>A. vaginae</i>, BVAB2, and <i>Megasphaera</i> type 1. This test has 90.5% sensitivity and 85.8% specificity for BV diagnosis, compared with Amsel criteria and Nugent score. It also provides results for <i>Candida</i> species and <i>T. vaginalis</i>. The Aptima BV detects <i>G. vaginalis</i>, <i>A. vaginae</i>, and certain <i>Lactobacillus</i> species including <i>L. crispatus</i>, <i>L. jensenii</i>, and <i>L. gasseri</i>, with sensitivity and specificity ranging from 95.0% to 97.3% and 85.8% to 89.6%, respectively (using either clinician- or patient-collected vaginal swabs). The three laboratory-developed tests (NuSwab VG, OneSwab BV Panel PCR with Lactobacillus Profiling by qPCR, and SureSwab BV) have to be internally validated before use for patient care yet have good sensitivity and specificity, similar to FDA-cleared assays. BV NAATs should be used among symptomatic women only (e.g., women with vaginal discharge, odor, or itch) because their accuracy is not well defined for asymptomatic women. Despite the availability of BV NAATs, traditional methods of BV diagnosis, including the Amsel criteria, Nugent score, and the Affirm VP III assay, remain useful for diagnosing symptomatic BV because of their lower cost and ability to provide a rapid diagnosis. Culture of <i>G. vaginalis</i> is not recommended as a diagnostic tool because it is not specific. Cervical Pap tests have no clinical utility for diagnosing BV because of their low sensitivity and specificity.</p>
<h4>Treatment</h4>
<p>Treatment for BV is recommended for women with symptoms. Established benefits of therapy among nonpregnant women are to relieve vaginal symptoms and signs of infection. Other potential benefits of treatment include reduction in the risk for acquiring <i>C. trachomatis, N. gonorrhoeae</i>, <i>T. vaginalis</i>, <i>M. genitalium</i>, HIV, HPV, and HSV-2 (<i>971</i>,<i>986</i>&ndash;<i>988</i>,<i>990</i>,<i>1010</i>). No data are available that directly compare the efficacy of oral and topical medications for treating BV.</p>
<table border="1px" cellpadding="5px">
<caption class="">Recommended Regimens for Bacterial Vaginosis</caption>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><b>Recommended Regimens for Bacterial Vaginosis</b></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><b>Metronidazole </b>500 mg orally 2 times/day for 7 days</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><i>or</i></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><b>Metronidazole gel 0.75%</b> one full applicator (5 g) intravaginally, once daily for 5 days</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><i>or</i></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><b>Clindamycin cream</b> <b>2%</b> one full applicator (5 g) intravaginally at bedtime for 7 days</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p>A review regarding alcohol consumption during metronidazole treatment reported no in vitro studies, animal models, reports of adverse effects, or clinical studies providing convincing evidence of a disulfiram-like interaction between alcohol and metronidazole (<i>1011</i>). The previous warning against simultaneous use of alcohol and metronidazole was based on laboratory experiments and individual case histories in which the reported reactions were equally likely to have been caused by alcohol alone or by adverse effects of metronidazole.</p>
<p>Metronidazole does not inhibit acetaldehyde dehydrogenase, as occurs with disulfiram. Ethanol alone or ethanol-independent side effects of metronidazole might explain the suspicion of disulfiram-like effects. Thus, refraining from alcohol use while taking metronidazole (or tinidazole) is unnecessary. Clindamycin cream is oil based and might weaken latex condoms and diaphragms for 5 days after use (refer to clindamycin product labeling for additional information).</p>
<p>Women should be advised to refrain from sexual activity or to use condoms consistently and correctly during the BV treatment regimen. Douching might increase the risk for relapse, and no data support use of douching for treatment or symptom relief.</p>
<table border="1px" cellpadding="5px">

<td><i>or</i></td>

<tr>
<td><i>or</i></td>

<td><b>Tinidazole</b> 2 g orally once daily for 2 days</td>
