<caption class="">Recommended Regimen for Adolescent and Adult Male Sexual Assault Survivors</caption>

<tr>
<td><b>Recommended Regimen for Adolescent and Adult Male Sexual Assault Survivors</b></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><b>Ceftriaxone</b> 500 mg* IM in a single dose</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><i>plus</i></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><b>Doxycycline </b>100 mg 2 times/day orally for 7 days</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>* For persons weighing &ge;150 kg, 1 g of ceftriaxone should be administered.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p>Clinicians should counsel persons regarding the possible benefits and toxicities associated with these treatment regimens; gastrointestinal side effects can occur with this combination. The efficacy of these regimens in preventing infections after sexual assault has not been evaluated. For those requiring alternative treatments, refer to the specific sections in this report relevant to the specific organisms.</p>
<h4>Other Management Considerations</h4>
<p>At the initial examination and, if indicated, at follow-up examinations, patients should be counseled regarding symptoms of STIs and the need for immediate examination if symptoms occur. Further, they should be instructed to abstain from sexual intercourse until STI prophylactic treatment is completed.</p>
<h4>Follow-Up</h4>
<p>After the initial postassault examination, follow-up examinations provide an opportunity to detect new infections acquired during or after the assault, complete hepatitis B and HPV vaccinations, if indicated, complete counseling and treatment for other STIs, and monitor side effects and adherence to PEP, if prescribed. If initial testing was performed, follow-up evaluation should be conducted in &lt;1 week to ensure that results of positive tests can be discussed promptly with the survivor, treatment is provided if not administered at the initial visit, and any follow-up for infections can be arranged. If initial tests are negative and treatment was not provided, examination for STIs can be repeated 1&ndash;2 weeks after the assault; repeat testing detects infectious organisms that might not have reached sufficient concentrations to produce positive test results at the time of initial examination. For survivors who are treated during the initial visit, regardless of whether testing was performed, posttreatment testing should be conducted only if the person reports having symptoms. If initial test results were negative and infection in the assailant cannot be ruled out, serologic tests for syphilis can be repeated at 4&ndash;6 weeks and 3 months; HIV testing can be repeated at 6 weeks and at 3 months by using methods to identify acute HIV infection.</p>
<h4>Risk for Acquiring HIV Infection</h4>
<p>HIV seroconversion has occurred among persons whose only known risk factor was sexual assault or sexual abuse; however, the frequency of this occurrence likely is low (<i>1428</i>,<i>1429</i>). In consensual sex, the per-act risk for HIV transmission from vaginal intercourse is 0.08%, and for receptive anal intercourse, 1.38% (<i>192</i>). The per-act risk for HIV transmission from oral sex is substantially lower. Specific circumstances of an assault (e.g., bleeding, which often accompanies trauma) might increase risk for HIV transmission in cases involving vaginal, anal, or oral penetration. Site of exposure to ejaculate, viral load in ejaculate, and the presence of an STI or genital lesions in the assailant or survivor also might increase risk for HIV acquisition.</p>
<p>PEP with a 28-day course of zidovudine was associated with an 81% reduction in risk for acquiring HIV in a study of health care workers who had percutaneous exposures to HIV-infected blood (<i>1430</i>). On the basis of these results and results from animal studies, PEP has been recommended for health care workers who have occupational exposures to HIV (<i>1431</i>). These findings have been extrapolated to nonoccupational injecting drug and sexual HIV exposures, including sexual assault. The possibility of HIV exposure from the assault should be assessed at the initial examination; survivors determined to be at risk for acquiring HIV should be informed about the possible benefit of PEP in preventing HIV infection. Initiation of PEP as soon as possible after the exposure increases the likelihood of prophylactic benefit.</p>
<p>Multiple factors affect the medical recommendation for PEP and affect the assault survivor&rsquo;s acceptance of that recommendation. These factors include the likelihood of the assailant having HIV, any exposure characteristics that might increase the risk for HIV transmission, the time elapsed after the event, and the potential benefits and risks associated with PEP (<i>1431</i>). Determination of the assailant&rsquo;s HIV status at the time of the postassault examination is usually not possible. Therefore, health care providers should assess any available information concerning the characteristics and HIV risk behaviors of the assailant (e.g., being an MSM or using injecting drugs), local epidemiology of HIV/AIDS, and exposure characteristics of the assault. When an assailant&rsquo;s HIV status is unknown, determinations about risk for HIV transmission to the survivor should be based on whether vaginal or anal penetration occurred; whether ejaculation occurred on mucous membranes; whether multiple assailants were involved; whether mucosal lesions were present in the assailant or survivor; and any other characteristics of the assault, survivor, or assailant that might increase risk for HIV transmission.</p>
<p>If PEP is offered, the following information should be discussed with the survivor: the necessity of early initiation of PEP to optimize potential benefits (i.e., as soon as possible after and &lt;72 hours after the assault), the importance of close follow-up, the benefit of adherence to recommended dosing, and potential adverse effects of antiretroviral medications. Providers should emphasize that severe adverse effects are rare from PEP (<i>1431</i>&ndash;<i>1435</i>). Clinical management of the survivor should be implemented according to the HIV PEP guidelines and in collaboration with specialists (<i>1436</i>). Health care providers should provide an initial course of 3&ndash;7 days of medication (i.e., a starter pack) with a prescription for the remainder of the course, or, if starter packs are unavailable, they should provide a prescription for an entire 28-day course. Provision of the entire 28-day PEP medication supply at the initial visit has been reported to increase likelihood of adherence, especially when patients have difficulty returning for multiple follow-up visits (<i>1437</i>). Routinely providing starter packs or the entire 28-day course requires that health care providers stock PEP drugs in their practice setting or have an established agreement with a pharmacy to stock, package, and urgently dispense PEP drugs with required administration instructions. Uninsured patients or those with high copayments can be enrolled in a patient-assistance program to ensure access to PEP medications. An early follow-up visit should be scheduled at which health care providers can discuss the results of HIV and STI testing, provide additional counseling and support, provide indicated vaccines not administered at the initial evaluation, assess medication side effects and adherence, or provide an altered PEP medication regimen if indicated by side effects or laboratory test results.</p>
<h4>Recommendations for Postexposure HIV Risk Assessment of Adolescents and Adults &lt;72 Hours After Sexual Assault</h4>
<p>Health care providers should do the following:</p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>Assess risk for HIV infection in the assailant, and test that person for HIV whenever possible.</li>
<li>Use the algorithm to evaluate the survivor for the need for HIV PEP (<a id="F1_up"></a><a href="#F1_down">Figure</a>) (<i>1436</i>).</li>
<li>Consult with a specialist in HIV treatment if PEP is being considered.</li>
<li>If the survivor appears to be at risk for acquiring HIV from the assault, discuss PEP, including benefits and risks.</li>
<li>If the survivor chooses to start PEP, provide an initial course of 3&ndash;7 days of medication (i.e., a starter pack) with a prescription for the remainder of the course or provide a prescription for an entire 28-day course. Schedule an early follow-up visit to discuss test results and provide additional counseling (<i>1438</i>).</li>
<li>If PEP is started, obtain serum creatinine, AST, and alanine aminotransferase at baseline.</li>
<li>Perform an HIV antibody test at original assessment; repeat at 6 weeks and 3 months.</li>
<li>Counsel the survivor regarding ongoing risk for HIV acquisition and about HIV PrEP, and provide referrals to a PrEP provider.</li>
</ul>
<p>Assistance with PEP-related decisions can be obtained by calling the National Clinician&rsquo;s Post Exposure Prophylaxis Hotline (PEP Line) (telephone: 888-448-4911).</p>
<h3 class="h3">Sexual Assault or Abuse of Children</h3>
<p>These guidelines are limited to the identification and treatment of STIs in prepubertal children. Management of the psychosocial or legal aspects of the sexual assault or abuse of children is beyond the scope of these guidelines.</p>
<p>Identification of STIs in children past the neonatal period strongly indicates sexual abuse (<i>1438</i>). The importance of identifying a sexually transmitted organism for such children as evidence of possible child sexual abuse varies by pathogen. Postnatally acquired gonorrhea, syphilis, chlamydia, and <i>T. vaginalis</i> infection and nontransfusion, nonperinatally acquired HIV infection are indicative of sexual abuse. Sexual abuse should be suspected when anogenital herpes or anogenital warts are diagnosed. Investigation of sexual abuse among children who have an infection that might have been transmitted sexually should be conducted in compliance with recommendations by clinicians who have experience and training in all elements of the evaluation of child abuse, neglect, and assault. The social significance of an infection that might have been acquired sexually varies by the specific organism, as does the threshold for reporting suspected child sexual abuse (<a id="T8_up"></a><a href="#T8_down">Table 8</a>). When any STI has been diagnosed in a child, efforts should be made in consultation with a specialist to evaluate the possibility of sexual abuse, including conducting a history and physical examination for evidence of abuse and diagnostic testing for other commonly occurring STIs (<i>1439</i>&ndash;<i>1441</i>).</p>
<p>The general rule that STIs beyond the neonatal period are evidence of sexual abuse has exceptions. For example, genital infection with <i>T. vaginalis</i> (<i>1442</i>) or rectal or genital infection with <i>C. trachomatis</i> among young children might be the result of perinatally acquired infection and has, in certain cases of chlamydial infection, persisted for as long as 2&ndash;3 years (<i>1443</i>&ndash;<i>1445</i>), although perinatal chlamydial infection is now uncommon because of prenatal screening and treatment of pregnant women. Genital warts have been diagnosed among children who have been sexually abused (<i>1426</i>) but also among children who have no other evidence of sexual abuse (<i>1446</i>,<i>1447</i>); lesions appearing for the first time in a child aged &gt;5 years are more likely to have been caused by sexual transmission (<i>1448</i>). BV has been diagnosed among children who have been abused but its presence alone does not prove sexual abuse. The majority of HBV infections among children result from household exposure to persons who have chronic HBV infection rather than sexual abuse.</p>
<h4>Reporting</h4>
<p>All U.S. states and territories have laws that require reporting of child abuse. Although the exact requirements differ by state or territory, if a health care provider has reasonable cause to suspect child abuse, a report must be made (<i>1448</i>). Health care providers should contact their state or local child protection service agency regarding child abuse reporting requirements.</p>
<h4>Evaluating Children for STIs</h4>
<p>Evaluating children for sexual assault or abuse should be conducted in a manner designed to minimize pain and trauma to the child. Examinations and collection of vaginal specimens in prepubertal girls can be extremely uncomfortable and should be performed by an experienced clinician to avoid psychological and physical trauma to the child. The decision to obtain genital or other specimens from a child to evaluate for STIs should be made on an individual basis. However, children who received a diagnosis of one STI should be screened for other STIs. History and reported type of sexual contact might not be a reliable indicator, and urogenital, pharyngeal, and rectal testing should be considered for preverbal children and children who cannot verbalize details of the assault (<i>1438</i>,<i>1449</i>). Factors that should lead the physician to consider testing for STIs include the following (<i>1449</i>):</p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>The child has experienced penetration or has evidence of recent or healed penetrative injury to the genitals, anus, or oropharynx.</li>
<li>The child has been abused by a stranger.</li>
<li>The child has been abused by an assailant known to be infected with an STI or at high risk for STIs (e.g., injecting drug user, MSM, person with multiple sex partners, or person with a history of STIs).</li>
<li>The child has a sibling, other relative, or another person in the household with an STI.</li>
<li>The child lives in an area with a high rate of STIs in the community.</li>
<li>The child has signs or symptoms of STIs (e.g., vaginal discharge or pain, genital itching or odor, urinary symptoms, or genital lesions or ulcers).</li>
<li>The child or parent requests STI testing.</li>
<li>The child is unable to verbalize details of the assault.</li>
</ul>
<p>If a child has symptoms, signs, or evidence of an infection that might be sexually transmitted, the child should be tested for common STIs before initiation of any treatment that might interfere with diagnosing other STIs. Because of the legal and psychosocial consequences of a false-positive diagnosis, only tests with high specificities should be used. The potential benefit to the child of a reliable STI diagnosis justifies deferring presumptive treatment until specimens for highly specific tests are obtained by providers with experience in evaluating sexually abused and assaulted children.</p>
<p>Evaluations should be performed on a case-by-case basis, according to history of assault or abuse and in a manner that minimizes the possibility for psychological trauma and social stigma. If the initial exposure was recent, the infectious organisms acquired through the exposure might not have produced sufficient concentrations to result in positive test results or examination findings (<i>1450</i>). Alternatively, positive test results after a recent exposure might represent the assailant&rsquo;s secretions (but would nonetheless be an indication for treatment of the child). A second visit approximately 2&ndash;6 weeks after the most recent sexual exposure should be scheduled to include a repeat physical examination and collection of additional specimens to identify any infection that might not have been detected at the time of initial evaluation. A single evaluation might be sufficient if the child was abused for an extended period and if a substantial amount of time elapsed between the last suspected episode of abuse and the medical evaluation. Compliance with follow-up appointments might be improved when law enforcement personnel or child protective services are involved.</p>
<h5>Initial Examination</h5>
<p>Visual inspection of the genital, perianal, and oral areas for genital discharge, odor, bleeding, irritation, warts, and ulcerative lesions should be performed during initial examination. The clinical manifestations of certain STIs are different for children than for adults. For example, typical vesicular lesions might be absent even in the presence of HSV infection. The following should be performed during the initial examination, if STI testing is indicated:</p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>Testing for <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> and <i>C. trachomatis</i> can be performed from specimens collected from the pharynx and rectum, as well as the vagina for girls and urine for boys. Cervical specimens are not recommended for prepubertal girls. For boys with a urethral discharge, a meatal specimen discharge is an adequate substitute for an intraurethral swab specimen. Culture or NAAT can be used to test for <i>N. gonorrhoeae </i>and <i>C. trachomatis.</i> Although data regarding NAAT for children are more limited and performance is test dependent (<i>553</i>), no evidence demonstrates that performance of NAAT for detection of <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> or <i>C. trachomatis</i> among children differs from that among adults. Only FDA-cleared NAAT assays should be used. Consultation with an expert is necessary before using NAAT in this context, both to minimize the possibility of cross-reaction with nongonococcal <i>Neisseria</i> species and other commensals (e.g., <i>N. meningitidis</i>, <i>N. sicca</i>, <i>N. lactamica</i>, <i>N. cinerea</i>, or <i>M. catarrhalis</i>) and to ensure correct interpretation of results. Because of the implications of a diagnosis of <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> or <i>C. trachomatis</i> infection in a child, only CLIA-validated, FDA-cleared NAATs should be used (<i>837</i>). If culture for the isolation of <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> or <i>C. trachomatis</i> is performed, only standard culture procedures should be followed. Specimens from the vagina, urethra, pharynx, or rectum should be streaked onto selective media for isolation of <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i>, and all presumptive isolates of <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> should be identified definitively by at least two tests that involve different approaches (e.g., biochemical, enzyme substrate, or molecular probes). Gram stains are inadequate for evaluating prepubertal children for gonorrhea and should not be used to diagnose or exclude gonorrhea. Specimens (either NAAT or culture, including any isolates) obtained before treatment should be preserved for further validation if needed. When a specimen is positive, the result should be confirmed either by retesting the original specimen or obtaining another. Because of the overall low prevalence of <i>N. gonorrhoeae</i> and <i>C. trachomatis</i> among children, false-positive results can occur, and all specimens that are initially positive should be confirmed.</li>
<li>Testing for <i>T. vaginalis</i> should not be limited to girls with vaginal discharge if other indications for vaginal testing exist because evidence indicates that asymptomatic sexually abused children might be infected with <i>T. vaginalis</i> and might benefit from treatment (<i>1451</i>,<i>1452</i>). NAAT can be used as an alternative or in addition to culture and wet mount, especially in settings where culture and wet mount of vaginal swab specimens are not obtainable. Data regarding use of NAATs for detection of <i>T. vaginalis</i> among children are limited; however, no evidence indicates that performance of NAAT for detection of <i>T. vaginalis</i> for children would differ from that for adults. Consultation with an expert is necessary before using NAAT in this context to ensure correct interpretation of results. Because of the implications of a diagnosis of <i>T. vaginalis</i> infection in a child, only CLIA-validated, FDA-cleared NAATs should be used (<i>837</i>). POC tests for <i>T. vaginalis</i> have not been validated for prepubertal children and should not be used. In the case of a positive specimen, the result should be confirmed either by retesting the original specimen or obtaining another. Because of the overall low prevalence of <i>T. vaginalis</i> among children, false-positive results can occur, and all specimens that are initially positive should be confirmed.</li>
<li>HSV can be indicative of sexual abuse; therefore, specimens should be obtained from all vesicular or ulcerative genital or perianal lesions and sent for NAAT or viral culture.</li>
<li>Wet mount can be used for a vaginal swab specimen for BV if discharge is present.</li>
<li>Collection of serum samples should be evaluated, preserved for subsequent analysis, and used as a baseline for comparison with follow-up serologic tests. Sera can be tested for antibodies to <i>T. pallidum</i>, HIV, and HBV. Decisions regarding the infectious agents for which to perform serologic tests should be made on a case-by-case basis.</li>
</ul>
<h4>Treatment</h4>
<p>The risk for a child acquiring an STI as a result of sexual abuse or assault has not been well studied. Presumptive treatment for children who have been sexually assaulted or abused is not recommended because the incidence of most STIs among children is low after abuse or assault, prepubertal girls appear to be at lower risk for ascending infection than adolescent or adult women, and regular follow-up of children usually can be ensured. However, certain children or their parent or guardian might be concerned about the possibility of infection with an STI, even if the health care provider has perceived the risk to be low. Such concerns might be an indication for presumptive treatment in certain settings and might be considered after all relevant specimens for diagnostic tests have been collected.</p>
<h4>Other Management Considerations</h4>
<p>Children who are survivors of sexual assault or abuse are at increased risk for future unsafe sexual practices that have been linked to higher risk for HPV acquisition (<i>1426</i>,<i>1453</i>) and are more likely to engage in these behaviors at an earlier age; therefore, ACIP recommends vaccination of these children at age &ge;9 years if they have not initiated or completed HPV vaccination (see Human Papillomavirus Infections, Prevention) (<a href="https://www.cdc.gov/vaccines/hcp/acip-recs/vacc-specific/hpv.html">https://www.cdc.gov/vaccines/hcp/acip-recs/vacc-specific/hpv.html</a>). Although HPV vaccine will not protect against progression of infection already acquired or promote clearance of the infection, the vaccine protects against HPV types not yet acquired.</p>
<h4>Follow-Up</h4>
<p>If no infections were identified at the initial examination after the last suspected sexual exposure, and if this exposure was recent, a follow-up evaluation approximately 2 weeks after the last exposure can be considered. Likewise, if no physical examination or diagnostic testing was performed at the initial visit, a complete examination can be scheduled approximately 2 weeks after the last exposure to identify any evidence of STIs. In circumstances in which transmission of syphilis, HIV, HBV, or HPV is a concern but baseline tests for syphilis, HIV, and HBV are negative and examinations for genital warts are negative, follow-up serologic testing and examination approximately 6 weeks and &lt;3 months after the last suspected sexual exposure is recommended to allow time for antibodies to develop and signs of infection to appear. In addition, results of HBsAg testing should be interpreted carefully because HBV can be transmitted nonsexually. Decisions regarding which tests should be performed should be made on a case-by-case basis.</p>
<h4>Risk for Acquiring HIV Infection</h4>
<p>HIV has been reported among children for whom sexual abuse was the only known risk factor. Serologic testing for HIV should be considered for sexually abused children. The decision to test for HIV should involve the family, if possible, and be made on a case-by-case basis depending on the likelihood of infection in the assailant (<i>1448</i>,<i>1454</i>). Although data are insufficient concerning the efficacy of PEP among children, treatment is well tolerated by infants and children with and without HIV, and children have a minimal risk for serious adverse reactions because of the short period recommended for prophylaxis (<i>1455</i>).</p>
<h4>Recommendations for Postexposure HIV Risk Assessment of Children &lt;72 Hours After Sexual Assault</h4>
<p>Providers should do the following:</p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>Review local HIV epidemiology, assess risk for HIV in the assailant, and test for HIV.</li>
<li>Evaluate the circumstances of the assault or abuse that might affect risk for HIV transmission.</li>
<li>Perform HIV antigen or antibody testing (or antibody testing, if antigen or antibody testing is unavailable) during the original assessment and again at follow-up visits, in accordance with CDC guidelines (<a href="https://stacks.cdc.gov/view/cdc/38856">https://stacks.cdc.gov/view/cdc/38856</a>). In considering whether to offer PEP, health care providers should consider whether the child can be treated soon after the sexual exposure (i.e., &lt;72 hours), the likelihood that the assailant has HIV infection, and the likelihood of high compliance with the prophylactic regimen (<i>1436</i>). Potential benefit of treating a sexually abused child should be weighed against the risk for adverse reactions.</li>
<li>Consult with a provider specializing in evaluating or treating children with HIV infection to determine age-appropriate dosing and regimens and baseline laboratory testing, if PEP is being considered.</li>
<li>Discuss PEP with the caregivers, including its toxicity, unknown efficacy, and possible benefits, for children determined to be at risk for HIV transmission from the assault or abuse.</li>
<li>Provided adequate doses of medication, if PEP is begun, to last until the follow-up visit 3&ndash;7 days after the initial assessment, at which time the child should be reevaluated and tolerance of medication assessed (<i>139</i>).</li>
</ul>
</div>

Bachmann, MD; Kyle Bernstein, PhD; Gail Bolan, MD; John Brooks, MD; Philip Chan, MD, Brown University, Providence, Rhode Island; Aaron Harris, MD; Matthew Hogben, PhD; Christine Johnston, MD, University of Washington, Seattle, Washington; Sarah Kidd, MD; Kristen Kreisel, PhD; Lauri Markowitz, MD; Christina Muzny, MD, University of Alabama at Birmingham, Birmingham, Alabama; Ina Park, MD, University of California San Francisco, San Francisco, California; Allan Pillay, PhD; Laura Quilter, MD; Hilary Reno, MD, PhD, Washington University, St. Louis, Missouri; Sancta St. Cyr, MD; Mona Saraiya, MD; Julie Schillinger, MD; Virginia Senkomago, PhD; Philip Spradling, MD; Phoebe Thorpe, MD; Mark Weng, MD; Jonathan Zenilman, MD, Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, Maryland.</p>

</div><div class="order-4"><p class="text-right"><a href="#">Top</a></p></div><div class="order-4 w-100"><h3><a id="Disclosure of Relationships" class="onThisPageAnchor" title="Disclosure of Relationships"></a>Disclosure of Relationships</h3>
<p>All STI Treatment Guidelines, 2021, Work Group members have completed and submitted the International Committee of Medical Journal Editors form for disclosure of potential conflicts of interest. Lindley Barbee received research support from Hologic and SpeeDx and consults for Nabriva. Christine Johnston received research funding from Sanofi-Pasteur; royalties from UpToDate; and personal fees from MedPace, Gilead, AbbVie, and UpToDate. Jeffrey Klausner received research supplies from Hologic, Cepheid, and SpeeDx; advisory fees from Shield Diagnostics, Click Diagnostics, and SpeeDx; and research funding from AbbVie and Gilead Sciences. Lisa Manhart received research supplies, honoraria, and research contracts from Hologic and served as a consultant to diagnostic test manufacturers (SpeeDx, BioFire Diagnostics, Roche Diagnostics, and Click Diagnostics). Jeanne Marrazzo serves on expert advisory boards for Becton Dickinson, BioFire Diagnostics, and Gilead Sciences and has research and grant support and supplies from Merck &amp; Co. and Toltec. David Martin served as a consultant in 2019 to Hologic, which recently gained Food and Drug Administration clearance for a <em>Mycoplasma genitalium</em> diagnostic test and markets diagnostic tests for gonorrhea, chlamydia, and trichomonas. Kenneth Mayer serves on the scientific advisory boards for Gilead Sciences and Merck &amp; Co. Pharmaceuticals and receives grant funds from Gilead Sciences. Leandro Mena received research funding through his institution from Atlas Genetics, Merck &amp; Co., Becton Dickinson, Hologic, Biolytical, and Roche and received consulting honoraria from Roche Molecular, Merck &amp; Co., Gilead Sciences, and ViiV Healthcare and from speaker bureaus for Gilead Sciences and ViiV Healthcare. Caroline Mitchell served as a consultant for Lupin Pharmaceuticals and Scynexis and received grant funding from Merck &amp; Co. Christina A. Muzny served as a consultant and received research support from Lupin Pharmaceuticals for a randomized controlled trial of secnidazole versus placebo for treatment of trichomoniasis and grants from the National Institutes of Health/National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases; was a speaker for Abbott Molecular, Cepheid, and Roche Diagnostics on topics related to STIs; received personal fees from Lupin Pharmaceuticals, PhagoMed, Cepheid, and Beckton Dickinson; and served as a consultant for BioFire Diagnostics. Paul Nyirjesy received research support from Mycovia Pharmaceuticals, Curatek Pharmaceuticals, Scynexis, and Hologic and served as a consultant for Mycovia Pharmaceuticals, Hologic, Scynexis, Dar&eacute; Bioscience, and Becton Dickinson. Jeffery Pepest serves on advisory boards for Cooper Surgical and Bayer and provides research support to Merck &amp; Co. Susan Philip is an unpaid public health advisor to GlaxoSmithKline. Anne Rompalo serves on the BioFire Diagnostics advisory board and has financial ties to UpToDate. Hilary Reno is a site principle investigator on a project evaluating the prevalence of <em>M. genitalium</em> funded by Hologic (funds are allocated via her institution). Arlene Se&ntilde;a serves on a speaker&rsquo;s bureau and scientific advisory board for <em>M. genitalium</em> at Hologic, works with the Gilead Focus grant for hepatitis C testing and linkage to care, and mentored Sancta St. Cyr, MD, at the University of North Carolina Division of Infectious Diseases before employment at CDC. Anne Spaulding received consulting fees or honoraria (either directly or through third parties) from Gilead Sciences, Merck &amp; Co., and AbbVie and grants through her institution from Gilead Sciences and ViiV. Susan Tuddenham served as a consultant for Biofire Diagnostics and Roche Molecular Diagnostics and received a speaker honorarium from Roche Molecular Diagnostics. Karen Wendel has stock ownership in Pfizer.</p>

</div><div class="order-4"><p class="text-right"><a href="#">Top</a></p></div><br><div class="order-4 w-100"><div class="footnote-text">
<p class="Footnote">* Regardless of condom use during exposure.</p>
<p class="Footnote"><sup>&dagger;</sup> Direct hyperbilirubinemia is direct bilirubin level &gt;2 mg/dL (34 umol/L) or 20% of the total bilirubin level.</p>
<p class="Footnote"><sup>&sect;</sup> One dilution is within the test performance of nontreponemal tests and is not a significant change.</p>
<p class="Footnote"><sup>&para;</sup> The absence of a fourfold or greater titer for a neonate does not exclude congenital syphilis.</p>
<p class="Footnote">** Interpretation of CSF test results requires a nontraumatic lumbar puncture (i.e., a CSF sample that is not contaminated with blood). CSF test results obtained during the neonatal period can be difficult to interpret; normal values differ by gestational age and are higher among preterm infants. Studies indicate that 95% of healthy neonates have values of &le;16&ndash;19 WBCs/mm<sup>3</sup> or protein levels of &le;115&ndash;118 mg/dL on CSF examination. During the second month of life, 95% of healthy infants have &le;9&ndash;11 WBCs/mm<sup>3</sup> or protein levels of &le;89&ndash;91 mg/dL. Lower values (i.e., 5 WBCs/mm<sup>3</sup> and protein level of 40 mg/dL) might be considered the upper limits of normal for older infants. Other causes of elevated values should be considered when an infant is being evaluated for congenital syphilis (<b>Sources:</b> Kestenbaum LA, Ebberson J, Zorc JJ, Hodinka RL, Shah SS. Defining cerebrospinal fluid white blood cell count reference values in neonates and young infants. Pediatrics 2010;125:257&ndash;64; Shah SS, Ebberson J, Kestenbaum LA, Hodinka RL, Zorc JJ. Age-specific reference values for cerebrospinal fluid protein concentration in neonates and young infants. J Hosp Med 2011;6:22&ndash;7; Thomson J, Sucharew H, Cruz AT, et al.; Pediatric Emergency Medicine Collaborative Research Committee [PEM CRC] HSV Study Group. Cerebrospinal fluid reference values for young infants undergoing lumbar puncture. Pediatrics 2018;141:e20173405.)</p>
<p class="Footnote"><sup>&dagger;&dagger;</sup> A women treated with a regimen other than recommended in these guidelines should be considered untreated.</p>
<p class="Footnote"><sup>&sect;&sect;</sup> For urethral microscopy, the cutoff for diagnosing urethritis is &ge;2 WBCs/HPF (<b>Sources:</b> Rietmeijer CA, Mettenbrink CJ. Recalibrating the Gram stain diagnosis of male urethritis in the era of nucleic acid amplification testing. Sex Transm Dis 2012;39:18&ndash;20; Rietmeijer CA, Mettenbrink CJ. The diagnosis of nongonococcal urethritis in men: can there be a universal standard? Sex Transm Dis 2017;44:195&ndash;6). An additional evaluation supported this cutoff by demonstrating NGU sensitivity of 92.6% for cutoff of &ge;2 versus 55.6% sensitivity for cutoff of &ge;5 (<b>Source:</b> Sarier M, Sepin N, Duman I, et al. Microscopy of Gram-stained urethral smear in the diagnosis of urethritis: which threshold value should be selected? Andrologia 2018;50:e13143). Diagnostic cutoffs for 369 symptomatic and asymptomatic heterosexual men seeking STI care in Seattle revealed a maximal sensitivity and specificity achieved with a cutoff of &ge;5 WBCs/HPF. Using a lower cutoff of &ge;2 WBCs/HPF would miss 13% of <i>C. trachomatis</i> and <i>M. genitalium</i> and overtreat 45% of persons who have negative tests (<b>Source:</b> Leipertz G, Chambers L, Lowens S, et al. P796 Reassessing the Gram stain smear [GSS] polymorphonuclear leukocyte [PMN] cutoff for diagnosing non-gonococcal urethritis [NGU]. Sex Transm Infect 2019;95[Suppl 1]:A339). Another study discussed that the WBC/HPF cutoff value should discriminate on the basis of the prevalence of chlamydia, mycoplasma, and gonorrhea among a clinic population (<b>Source:</b> Moi H, Hartgill U, Skullerud KH, Reponen EJ, Syvertsen L, Moghaddam A. Microscopy of stained urethral smear in male urethritis: which cutoff should be used? Sex Transm Dis 2017;44:189&ndash;94).</p>

<li>Gavril AR, Kellogg ND, Nair P. Value of follow-up examinations of children and adolescents evaluated for sexual abuse and assault. Pediatrics 2012;129:282&ndash;9. <a href="http://doi.org/10.1542/peds.2011-0804" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer" class="tp-link-policy" data-domain-ext="org">https://doi.org/10.1542/peds.2011-0804<span aria-label="external icon" class="sr-only" role="img"></span><span class="fi cdc-icon-external x16 fill-external" alt="" aria-hidden="true"></span></a> <a href="http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22291113" target="_blank" class="tp-link-policy" rel="noreferrer" data-domain-ext="gov">PMID:22291113<span aria-label="external icon" class="sr-only" role="img"></span><span class="fi cdc-icon-external x16 fill-external" alt="" aria-hidden="true"></span></a></li>
<li>Bandea CI, Joseph K, Secor EW, et al. Development of PCR assays for detection of <i>Trichomonas vaginalis</i> in urine specimens. J Clin Microbiol 2013;51:1298&ndash;300. <a href="http://doi.org/10.1128/JCM.03101-12" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer" class="tp-link-policy" data-domain-ext="org">https://doi.org/10.1128/JCM.03101-12<span aria-label="external icon" class="sr-only" role="img"></span><span class="fi cdc-icon-external x16 fill-external" alt="" aria-hidden="true"></span></a> <a href="http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23390274" target="_blank" class="tp-link-policy" rel="noreferrer" data-domain-ext="gov">PMID:23390274<span aria-label="external icon" class="sr-only" role="img"></span><span class="fi cdc-icon-external x16 fill-external" alt="" aria-hidden="true"></span></a></li>
<li>Gallion HR, Dupree LJ, Scott TA, Arnold DH. Diagnosis of <i>Trichomonas vaginalis</i> in female children and adolescents evaluated for possible sexual abuse: a comparison of the InPouch TV culture method and wet mount microscopy. J Pediatr Adolesc Gynecol 2009;22:300&ndash;5. <a href="http://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpag.2008.12.006" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer" class="tp-link-policy" data-domain-ext="org">https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpag.2008.12.006<span aria-label="external icon" class="sr-only" role="img"></span><span class="fi cdc-icon-external x16 fill-external" alt="" aria-hidden="true"></span></a> <a href="http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19576816" target="_blank" class="tp-link-policy" rel="noreferrer" data-domain-ext="gov">PMID:19576816<span aria-label="external icon" class="sr-only" role="img"></span><span class="fi cdc-icon-external x16 fill-external" alt="" aria-hidden="true"></span></a></li>
<li>Lalor K, McElvaney R. Child sexual abuse, links to later sexual exploitation/high-risk sexual behavior, and prevention/treatment programs. Trauma Violence Abuse 2010;11:159&ndash;77. <a href="http://doi.org/10.1177/1524838010378299" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer" class="tp-link-policy" data-domain-ext="org">https://doi.org/10.1177/1524838010378299<span aria-label="external icon" class="sr-only" role="img"></span><span class="fi cdc-icon-external x16 fill-external" alt="" aria-hidden="true"></span></a> <a href="http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20679329" target="_blank" class="tp-link-policy" rel="noreferrer" data-domain-ext="gov">PMID:20679329<span aria-label="external icon" class="sr-only" role="img"></span><span class="fi cdc-icon-external x16 fill-external" alt="" aria-hidden="true"></span></a></li>
<li>Girardet RG, Lemme S, Biason TA, Bolton K, Lahoti S. HIV post-exposure prophylaxis in children and adolescents presenting for reported sexual assault. Child Abuse Negl 2009;33:173&ndash;8. <a href="http://doi.org/10.1016/j.chiabu.2008.05.010" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer" class="tp-link-policy" data-domain-ext="org">https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chiabu.2008.05.010<span aria-label="external icon" class="sr-only" role="img"></span><span class="fi cdc-icon-external x16 fill-external" alt="" aria-hidden="true"></span></a> <a href="http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19324415" target="_blank" class="tp-link-policy" rel="noreferrer" data-domain-ext="gov">PMID:19324415<span aria-label="external icon" class="sr-only" role="img"></span><span class="fi cdc-icon-external x16 fill-external" alt="" aria-hidden="true"></span></a></li>

Washington, DC: US Department of Health and Human Services, National Institutes of Health, AIDSinfo; 2020. <a href="https://aidsinfo.nih.gov/contentfiles/lvguidelines/pediatricguidelines.pdf" target="_blank" class="tp-link-policy" rel="noreferrer" data-domain-ext="gov">https://aidsinfo.nih.gov/contentfiles/lvguidelines/pediatricguidelines.pdf<span aria-label="pdf icon" class="sr-only" role="img"></span><span class="fi cdc-icon-pdf x16 fill-pdf" alt="" aria-hidden="true"></span><span class="file-details"></span><span aria-label="external icon" class="sr-only" role="img"></span><span class="fi cdc-icon-external x16 fill-external" alt="" aria-hidden="true"></span></a></li>
</ol>
</div><div class="order-4"><p class="text-right"><a href="#">Top</a></p></div><div class="card order-4 w-100"><div class="card-body"><h5 class="h5"><a id="B1_down"></a><a href="#B1_up"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="float-right" title="Return to your place in the text" src="//www.cdc.gov/images/arrow_up.gif" alt="Return to your place in the text" width="58" height="34" border="0"></a><b>BOX 1</b>. <strong>The Five P&rsquo;s approach for health care providers obtaining sexual histories: partners, practices, protection from sexually transmitted infections, past history of sexually transmitted infections, and pregnancy intention</strong></h5>
<p><b>1. Partners</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>&ldquo;Are you currently having sex of any kind?&rdquo;</li>
<li>&ldquo;What is the gender(s) of your partner(s)?&rdquo;</li>
</ul>
<p><b>2. Practices</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>&ldquo;To understand any risks for sexually transmitted infections (STIs), I need to ask more specific questions about the kind of sex you have had recently.&rdquo;</li>
<li>&ldquo;What kind of sexual contact do you have or have you had?&rdquo;<br>
&ordm;&ldquo;Do you have vaginal sex, meaning &lsquo;penis in vagina&rsquo; sex?&rdquo;<br>
&ordm;&ldquo;Do you have anal sex, meaning &lsquo;penis in rectum/anus&rsquo; sex?&rdquo;<br>
&ordm;&ldquo;Do you have oral sex, meaning &lsquo;mouth on penis/vagina&rsquo;?&rdquo;</li>
</ul>
<p><b>3. Protection from STIs</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>&ldquo;Do you and your partner(s) discuss prevention of STIs and human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)?&rdquo;</li>
<li>&ldquo;Do you and your partner(s) discuss getting tested?&rdquo;</li>
<li>For condoms:<br>
&ordm; &ldquo;What protection methods do you use? In what situations do you use condoms?&rdquo;</li>
</ul>
<p><b>4. Past history of STIs</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>&ldquo;Have you ever been tested for STIs and HIV?&rdquo;</li>
<li>&ldquo;Have you ever been diagnosed with an STI in the past?&rdquo;</li>
<li>&ldquo;Have any of your partners had an STI?&rdquo;</li>
</ul>
<p><strong>Additional questions for identifying HIV and viral hepatitis risk:</strong></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>&ldquo;Have you or any of your partner(s) ever injected drugs?&rdquo;</li>
<li>&ldquo;Is there anything about your sexual health that you have questions about?&rdquo;</li>
</ul>
<p><b>5. Pregnancy intention</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>&ldquo;Do you think you would like to have (more) children in the future?&rdquo;</li>
<li>&ldquo;How important is it to you to prevent pregnancy (until then)?&rdquo;</li>
<li>&ldquo;Are you or your partner using contraception or practicing any form of birth control?&rdquo;</li>
<li>&ldquo;Would you like to talk about ways to prevent pregnancy?&rdquo;</li>
</ul>
</div></div><div class="order-4"><p class="text-right"><a href="#">Top</a></p></div><div class="card order-4 w-100"><div class="card-body"><h5 class="h5"><a id="B2_down"></a><a href="#B2_up"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="float-right" title="Return to your place in the text" src="//www.cdc.gov/images/arrow_up.gif" alt="Return to your place in the text" width="58" height="34" border="0"></a><b>BOX 2</b>. <strong>Low-risk history in patients who report penicillin allergy</strong></h5>
<p><strong>Gastrointestinal symptoms</strong></p>
<p>Headache</p>
<p>Pruritis without rash</p>
<p>Localized rash</p>
<p>Delayed onset rash (&gt;24 hours)</p>
<p>Symptoms unknown</p>
<p>Family history of penicillin or another drug allergy</p>
<p>Patient denies allergy but it is on the medical record</p>
</div></div><div class="order-4"><p class="text-right"><a href="#">Top</a></p></div><div class="card order-4 w-100"><div class="card-body"><h5 class="h5"><a id="B3_down"></a><a href="#B3_up"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="float-right" title="Return to your place in the text" src="//www.cdc.gov/images/arrow_up.gif" alt="Return to your place in the text" width="58" height="34" border="0"></a><b>BOX 3</b>. <strong>Skin test reagents for identifying persons at risk for adverse reactions to penicillin</strong></h5>
<p><b>Major determinant</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>Benzylpenicilloyl polylysine injection (Pre-Pen) (AllerQuest) (6 &times; 10<sup>-5</sup>M)</li>
</ul>
<p><b>Minor determinant precursors</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>Benzylpenicillin G (10<sup>-2</sup>M, 3.3 mg/mL, 10,000 units/mL)</li>
<li>Benzylpenicilloate (10<sup>-2</sup>M, 3.3 mg/mL)</li>
<li>Benzylpenicilloate (or penicilloyl propylamine) (10<sup>-2</sup>M, 3.3 mg/mL)</li>
</ul>
<p><strong>Aged penicillin is not an adequate source of minor determinants. Penicillin G should either be freshly prepared or come from a fresh-frozen source.</strong></p>
<p><b>Positive control</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>Commercial histamine for scratch testing (1.0 mg/mL)</li>
</ul>
<p><b>Negative control</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>Diluent (usually saline) or allergen diluent</li>
</ul>
<p><b>Source:</b> Adapted from Saxon A, Beall GN, Rohr AS, Adelman DC. Immediate hypersensitivity reactions to beta-lactam antibiotics. Ann Intern Med 1987;107:204&minus;15.</p>
</div></div><div class="order-4"><p class="text-right"><a href="#">Top</a></p></div><div class="card order-4 w-100"><div class="card-body"><h5 class="h5"><a id="B4_down"></a><a href="#B4_up"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="float-right" title="Return to your place in the text" src="//www.cdc.gov/images/arrow_up.gif" alt="Return to your place in the text" width="58" height="34" border="0"></a><b>BOX 4</b>. <strong>Classification of vulvovaginal candidiasis</strong></h5>
<p><b>Uncomplicated vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC)</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>Sporadic or infrequent VVC<br>
<b><i>and</i></b></li>
<li>Mild-to-moderate VVC<br>
<b><i>and</i></b></li>
<li>Likely to be <i>Candida albicans<br>
</i><b><i>and</i></b></li>
<li>Nonimmunocompromised women</li>
</ul>
<p><b>Complicated VVC</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>Recurrent VVC (three or more episodes of symptomatic VVC in &lt;1 year)<br>
<b><i>or</i></b></li>
<li>Severe VVC<br>
<b><i>or</i></b></li>
<li>Non&ndash;<i>albicans</i> candidiasis<br>
<b><i>or</i></b></li>
<li>Women with diabetes, immunocompromising conditions (e.g., HIV infection), underlying immunodeficiency, or immunosuppressive therapy (e.g., corticosteroids)</li>
</ul>
<p><b>Source:</b> Sobel JD, Faro S, Force RW, et al.&nbsp;Vulvovaginal candidiasis: epidemiologic, diagnostic, and therapeutic considerations.&nbsp;Am J Obstet Gynecol 1998;178:203&ndash;11.</p>

<p class="caption"><b><b>Sources: </b></b>Massad LS, Einstein MH, Huh WK, et al.; 2012 ASCCP Consensus Guidelines Conference. 2012 updated consensus guidelines for the management of abnormal cervical cancer screening tests and cancer precursors. Obstet Gynecol 2013;121:829&ndash;46; Perkins RB, Guido RS, Castle PE, et al; 2019 ASCCP Risk-Based Management Consensus Guidelines Committee. 2019 ASCCP risk-based management consensus guidelines for abnormal cervical cancer screening tests and cancer precursors. J Low Genit Tract Dis 2020;24:102&ndash;31; Perkins R, Guido R, Saraiya M, et al. Summary of current guidelines for cervical cancer screening and management of abnormal test results: 2016&ndash;2020. J Womens Health (Larchmt) 2021;30:5&ndash;13.<br>
<b><b>Abbreviations:</b></b> AGC = atypical glandular cells; AIS = adenocarcinoma in situ; ASC-H = atypical squamous cells cannot exclude high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion; ASC-US = atypical squamous cells of undetermined significance; CIN = cervical intraepithelial neoplasia; HPV = human papillomavirus; HSIL = high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion; LSIL = low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion; NILM = negative for intraepithelial lesion or malignancy; Pap = Papanicolaou.<br>
* Colposcopy may be warranted for patients with a history of high-grade lesions (CIN 2 or CIN 3, histologic or cytologic HSIL, ASC-H, AGC, or AIS).<br>
<sup>&dagger;</sup> Previous Pap test results do not modify the recommendation; colposcopy is always recommended for two consecutive HPV-positive tests<br>
<sup>&sect; </sup>Negative HPV test or cotest (HPV plus Pap test) results only reduce risk sufficiently to defer colposcopy if performed for screening purposes within the last 5 years. Colposcopy is still warranted if negative HPV test or cotest results occurred in the context of surveillance for a previous abnormal result.<br>
<sup>&para; </sup>Expedited treatment is preferred for nonpregnant patients aged &ge;25 years. Colposcopy with biopsy is an acceptable option if desired by patient after shared decision-making.</p>

</div><div class="order-4"><p class="text-right"><a href="#">Top</a></p></div><div class="card order-4 w-100"><div class="card-body"><h5 class="h5"><a id="B5_down"></a><a href="#B5_up"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="float-right" title="Return to your place in the text" src="//www.cdc.gov/images/arrow_up.gif" alt="Return to your place in the text" width="58" height="34" border="0"></a><b>BOX 5</b>. <strong>Populations recommended for hepatitis A vaccination &mdash; Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices, 2020</strong></h5>
<p><b>Children</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>All children aged 12&ndash;23 months</li>
<li>Unvaccinated children and adolescents aged 2&ndash;18 years</li>
</ul>
<p><b>Persons at increased risk for hepatitis A virus (HAV) infection</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>International travelers</li>
<li>Men who have sex with men</li>
<li>Persons who use injecting or noninjecting drugs (i.e., all those who use illegal drugs)</li>
<li>Persons with occupational risk for exposure</li>
<li>Persons who anticipate close personal contact with an international adoptee</li>
<li>Persons experiencing homelessness</li>
</ul>
<p><b>Persons at increased risk for severe disease from HAV infection</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>Persons with chronic liver disease</li>
<li>Persons with HIV infection</li>
</ul>
<p><b>Other persons recommended for vaccination</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>Pregnant women at risk for HAV infection or severe outcome from HAV infection</li>
<li>Any persons who requests a vaccine</li>
</ul>
<p><b>Vaccination during outbreaks</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>Unvaccinated persons in outbreak settings who are at risk for HAV infection or at risk for severe disease from HAV</li>
</ul>
<p><b>Implementation strategies for settings providing services to adults</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>Persons in settings that provide services to adults where a high proportion of those persons have risk factors for HAV infection</li>
</ul>
<p><b>Hepatitis A vaccination is no longer recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices</b></p>
<ul class="list-bullet">
<li>Persons who receive blood products for clotting disorders (e.g., hemophilia)</li>
</ul>
<p><b>Source</b>: Nelson NP, Weng MK, Hofmeister MG, et al. Prevention of hepatitis A virus infection in the United States: recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices, 2020. MMWR Recomm Rep 2020;69(No. RR-5).</p>

<p class="caption"><b><b>Source:</b></b> Nelson NP, Weng MK, Hofmeister MG, et al. Prevention of hepatitis A virus infection in the United States: recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices, 2020. MMWR Recomm Rep 2020;69(No. RR-5).<br>
<b><b>Abbreviations:</b></b> HAV = hepatitis A virus; IG = immune globulin.<br>
* Measles, mumps, and rubella vaccine should not be administered for &ge;2 weeks before and 6 months after administration of IG.<br>
<sup>&dagger; </sup>A second dose of hepatitis A vaccine is not required for postexposure prophylaxis; however, for long-term immunity, the vaccination series should be completed with a second dose &ge;6 months after the first dose.<br>
<sup>&sect; </sup>The provider&rsquo;s risk assessment should determine the need for IG administration. If the provider&rsquo;s risk assessment determines that both vaccine and IG are warranted, hepatitis A vaccine and IG should be administered simultaneously at different anatomic sites (e.g., separate limbs).<br>
<sup>&para; </sup>Vaccine and IG should be administered simultaneously at different anatomic sites (e.g., separate limbs).<br>
** Life-threatening allergic reaction to a previous dose of hepatitis A vaccine or allergy to any vaccine component.<br>
<sup>&dagger;&dagger; </sup>IG should be considered before travel for persons with special risk factors for either HAV infection or severe disease from HAV infection.<br>
<sup>&sect;&sect; </sup>0.1 mL/kg body weight for travel &le;1 month; 0.2 mL/kg body weight for travel &le;2 months; 0.2 mL/kg every 2 months for travel of &ge;2 months&rsquo; duration.<br>
<sup>&para;&para; </sup>This dose should not be counted toward the routine 2-dose series, which should be initiated at age 12 months.<br>
*** For persons not previously vaccinated with hepatitis A vaccine, administer dose as soon as travel is considered and complete the series according to routine schedule if the next dose is needed before travel.<br>
<sup>&dagger;&dagger;&dagger; </sup>Can be administered on the basis of the provider&rsquo;s risk assessment.</p>

<p class="caption"><b><b>Source: </b></b>Adapted from Schillie S, Vellozzi C, Reingold A, et al. Prevention of hepatitis B virus infection in the United States: recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices. MMWR Recomm Rep 2018;67(No. RR-1).<br>
<b><b>Abbreviation:</b></b> NA = not applicable.<br>
* Administered on a 2-dose schedule.<br>
<sup>&dagger; </sup>Combined hepatitis A and B vaccines. This vaccine is recommended for persons aged &ge;18 years who are at increased risk for both hepatitis B and hepatitis A virus infections.<br>
<sup>&sect; </sup>Recombinant hepatitis B surface antigen protein dose.<br>
<sup>&para;</sup> Heplisav-B should not be used for vaccination of infants, children, or adolescents because the safety and effectiveness of Heplisav-B has not been established in persons aged &lt;8 years and is not approved for use in these populations.<br>
** Adult formulation administered on a 2-dose schedule.<br>
<sup>&dagger;&dagger;</sup> Engerix-B and Recombivax HB are approved for use in persons of all ages.<br>
<sup>&sect;&sect; </sup>Higher doses might be more immunogenic; however, no specific recommendations have been made.<br>
<sup>&para;&para; </sup>Dialysis formulation administered on a 3-dose schedule at 0, 1, and 6 months.<br>
*** Two 1.0-mL doses administered at one site, on a 4-dose schedule at 0, 1, 2, and 6 months.</p>
</div><div class="order-4"><p class="text-right"><a href="#">Top</a></p></div><div class="order-4 w-100"><table class="table table-bordered table-responsive">
<caption class="caption-top h4 text-black" style="text-align: left"><a id="T7_down"></a>
<h5><strong>TABLE 7. Guidelines for postexposure prophylaxis* of persons with nonoccupational exposure<sup>&dagger; </sup>to blood or body fluids that contain blood, by exposure type and hepatitis B vaccination status</strong><a href="#T7_up"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="float-right" title="Return to your place in the text" src="//www.cdc.gov/images/arrow_up.gif" alt="Return to your place in the text" width="58" height="34" border="0"></a></h5>
</caption>
<thead>
<tr>
<th class="row1left" scope="col" align="left" valign="top">Source of exposure</th>
<th class="row1" scope="col" align="left" valign="top">Unvaccinated person<sup>&sect;</sup></th>
<th class="row1" scope="col" align="left" valign="top">Previously vaccinated person<sup>&para;</sup></th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td align="left" valign="top"><b><b>HBsAg-positive source<br>
</b></b>Percutaneous (e.g., bite or needlestick) or mucosal exposure to HBsAg-positive blood or body fluids<br>
<b><b><i><i>or<br>
</i></i></b></b>Sex or needle-sharing contact with an HBsAg-positive person<br>
<b><b><i><i>or<br>
</i></i></b></b>Victim of sexual assault or abuse by an assailant who is HBsAg positive</td>
<td align="left" valign="top">Administer hepatitis B vaccine series and HBIG</td>
<td align="left" valign="top">Complete hepatitis B vaccine series and HBIG, if vaccine series not completed<br>
<b><b><i><i>or<br>
</i></i></b></b>Administer hepatitis B vaccine booster dose, if previous vaccination without testing**</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td align="left" valign="top"><b><b>Source with unknown HBsAg status<br>
</b></b>Percutaneous (e.g., bite or needlestick) or mucosal exposure to potentially infectious blood or body fluids from a source with unknown HBsAg status<br>
<b><b><i><i>or<br>
</i></i></b></b>Sex or needle-sharing contact with person with unknown HBsAg status<br>
<b><b><i><i>or<br>
</i></i></b></b>Victim of sexual assault or abuse by a perpetrator with unknown HBsAg status</td>
<td align="left" valign="top">Administer hepatitis B vaccine series</td>
<td align="left" valign="top">Complete hepatitis B vaccine series</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p class="caption"><b><b>Sources:</b></b> CDC. CDC guidance for evaluating health-care personnel for hepatitis B virus protection and for administering postexposure management. MMWR Recomm Rep 2013;62(No. RR-10); CDC. Postexposure prophylaxis to prevent hepatitis B virus infection. MMWR Recomm Rep 2006;55(No. RR-16).<br>
<b><b>Abbreviations:</b></b> HBIG = hepatitis B immune globulin; HBsAg = hepatitis B surface antigen.<br>
* When indicated, immunoprophylaxis should be initiated as soon as possible, preferably within 24 hours. Studies are limited regarding the maximum interval after exposure during which postexposure prophylaxis is effective, but the interval is unlikely to exceed 7 days for percutaneous exposures or 14 days for sexual exposures. The hepatitis B vaccine series should be completed. These guidelines apply to nonoccupational exposures.<br>
<sup>&dagger; </sup>These guidelines apply to nonoccupational exposures.<br>
<sup>&sect; </sup>A person who is in the process of being vaccinated but who has not completed the vaccine series should complete the series and receive treatment for hepatitis B as indicated.<br>
<sup>&para; </sup>A person who has written documentation of a complete hepatitis B vaccine series and who did not receive postvaccination testing.<br>
** No booster dose is needed for persons who have written documentation of hepatitis B vaccine series with serologic response.</p>
</div><div class="order-4"><p class="text-right"><a href="#">Top</a></p></div><div class="order-4 w-100"><div class="col-12"></div>
<h5 class="h5"><a id="F1_down"></a><a href="#F1_up"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" class="float-right" title="Return to your place in the text" src="//www.cdc.gov/images/arrow_up.gif" alt="Return to your place in the text" width="58" height="34" border="0"></a><b>FIGURE</b>. <strong>Algorithm to evaluate the need for nonoccupational HIV postexposure prophylaxis among adult and adolescent survivors of sexual assault</strong></h5>
<img decoding="async" class="img-fluid" alt="Figure is an algorithm for health care providers to follow when evaluating adult and adolescent survivors of sexual assault for the need for nonoccupational HIV postexposure prophylaxis." title="Sexually Transmitted Infections Treatment  Guidelines, 2021" src="/mmwr/volumes/70/rr/figures/rr7004a1-F.gif?_=90765">
<p><b>Source:</b> Adapted from Announcement: updated guidelines for antiretroviral postexposure prophylaxis after sexual, injection-drug use, or other nonoccupational exposure to HIV&mdash;United States, 2016. MMWR Morb Mortal Wkly Rep 2016;65:458.<br>
<b>Abbreviation:</b> PEP = postexposure prophylaxis.</p>

<script>var MP = {
<!--mp_trans_disable_start -->
	Version: '3.1.1.1',
	SrcLang: 'en',
<!--mp_trans_disable_end -->
	UrlLang: 'mp_js_current_lang',
	SrcUrl: decodeURIComponent('mp_js_orgin_url'),
	oSite: decodeURIComponent('mp_js_origin_baseUrl'),
	tSite: decodeURIComponent('mp_js_translated_baseUrl'),
<!--mp_trans_disable_start -->
	init: function() {
		if (MP.oSite.indexOf('p_js_') == 1) {
			MP.SrcUrl = window.top.document.location.href;
			MP.oSite = MP.tSite = window.top.document.location.host;
			MP.UrlLang = MP.SrcLang;
		}
	},
	switchLanguage: function(url,pref,sync) {
		var sync = sync;
		var oSite=MP.oSite.replace('http://','').replace('https://','').replace(/\/?$/, '');
		var tSite=MP.tSite.replace('http://','').replace('https://','').replace(/\/?$/, '');
		url=url.replace('http://','').replace('https://','').replace(/\/?$/, '');
		if(sync && (typeof MpStorage !== 'undefined')&&(typeof MpStorage.updatePref !== 'undefined')){
			if(pref){
			var lang = pref.substring(0, 2);
			pref = lang == MP.UrlLang ? MP.SrcLang+pref.substring(2):pref;
			}
			MpStorage.updatePref(url,pref);
		}
		setTimeout(function() {
			var script = document.createElement('SCRIPT');
			if (url == oSite) {
				tSite = tSite.split(/[/?#]/)[0];
				script.src = location.protocol + '//' + tSite +'?1023749634;' + encodeURIComponent(location.href);
			} else {
				url = url.split(/[/?#]/)[0];
				script.src = location.protocol + '//' + url +'?1023749632;' + encodeURIComponent(MP.SrcUrl);
			}
			var target = document.getElementsByTagName('script')[0];
			target.parentNode.insertBefore(script, target);
		}, 500);
		return false;
	},
	switchToLang: function(url) {
		if(window.top.location.href == url){
			if((typeof MpStorage !== 'undefined')&&(typeof MpStorage.updatePref !== 'undefined'))
			MpStorage.updatePref(MP.oSite,MP.SrcLang);
		}else{
			window.top.location.href = url;
		}
	}
